title: Alcohol poisoning: Hey, are you ok? (poster) - Canada.ca
content:
## Hey, are you ok? (poster)
Download in PDF format (197 KB, 1 page)
Organization: Public Health Agency of Canada
Too many alcohol overdoses aren't addressed because the difference between being really drunk and an overdose can sometimes be hard to identify.
Know the signs: C.U.P.S .
- C old and sweaty skin
- S low/irregular breathing or heart rate
If your friend or someone around you is experiencing any of these symptoms, they may need immediate medical attention. Remember: If you ask for help and you have other drugs on you, you can be protected by the Good Samaritan Law.
- Check in with the person by talking to them or gently shaking them.
- If they are not responsive, call for help (campus services, security, 911).
- Sit them down, or if they are unable to sit up, lay them on their side in the recovery position ( Figure 1 ) to prevent choking.
- Do not leave them alone.
- Try to find out if they've taken other substances and how much.
A person putting someone into the recovery position. The features of the recovery position include:
- lying down on left side
- airway is kept clear with head supported by right hand
- right knee bent to prevent rolling onto stomach
For more tips: How young adults can reduce risks related to alcohol use
